region_of_origin: Tamil Nadu, India; widely prepared by Tamil community in Singapore
food_subgroup: Deep-fried rice flour and jaggery sweet; thick disc-shaped festive confection

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Adhirasam. Closest parent is deep-fried rice flour sweet (FOODON:03315595 — fried food product). No exact URI available; null used honestly.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fried dough confectionery — closest FoodEx2 parent for deep-fried rice flour sweet; no Adhirasam-specific code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No Adhirasam-specific FoodEx2 code. A039S (fried dough confectionery) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Adhirasam or equivalent traditional South Indian fried sweet.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Adhirasam piece weighs approximately 50 g and is the typical serving size consumed during Deepavali celebrations.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Adhirasam
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Adhirasam is listed directly in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods table. Values adopted as reported. ICMR-NIN is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Flour and Jaggery-Based Confections — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice flour products (GI ~75–80) and jaggery (GI ~68)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Adhirasam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~70 (high) based on: (1) rice flour base GI ~75–80 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice flour products; (2) jaggery GI ~68 per published peer-reviewed literature (Bhatt et al., 2021, Journal of Food Science and Technology); (3) deep-frying fat content moderately attenuates glycemic response. Blended estimate of ~70 is conservative and defensible. GL per 50g serving = (70 × 32.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.75, rounded to 22.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (20 mg per 100g; ~10 mg per 50g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Adhirasam is not a sodium concern for hypertensive patients; one piece (50g) contributes only ~10 mg sodium, well below the 2000 mg daily limit, though overall caloric density should be considered in weight management for blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) and high glycemic load per piece (~22.8); jaggery GI (~68) is nearly equivalent to white sugar (~65)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Adhirasam is not suitable for T2DM patients in regular consumption — one piece (50g) delivers ~32.5g available carbohydrate with a GL of ~23 (equivalent to a bowl of white rice); the widespread belief that jaggery is diabetes-safe is clinically incorrect as jaggery's GI (~68) is nearly identical to white sugar, and patients should limit intake to at most half a piece (25g, GL ~11) on significant festive occasions only.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (38g/100g), moderate fat from deep-frying (12g/100g), low saturated fat (2g/100g); sesame oil used in frying contains beneficial lignans
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sugar load and caloric density (380 kcal/100g) make Adhirasam a food to limit for cardiovascular disease patients; saturated fat is relatively low (2g/100g) and sesame oil provides minor anti-inflammatory sesamin lignans, but frequent consumption contributes to excess caloric and sugar intake that adversely affects cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content promotes advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation; sesame oil provides minor anti-inflammatory sesamin lignans
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high jaggery-derived sugar content in Adhirasam promotes AGE formation which can exacerbate inflammatory pathways in rheumatoid arthritis; sesame oil used in frying contains sesamin lignans with minor anti-inflammatory properties, but this benefit is outweighed by the overall pro-inflammatory high-sugar, high-carbohydrate profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Adhirasam (அதிரசம்) is one of the oldest recorded Tamil sweet recipes, first referenced in Sangam-era Tamil literature approximately 2000 years ago, making it among the most ancient documented recipes in the world. It is a thick, golden-brown disc of deep-fried rice flour dough sweetened with jaggery (vellam — unrefined cane or palm sugar) and fragrant with cardamom. The preparation involves soaking raw rice, drying it, grinding it to a fine flour, then combining it with melted jaggery syrup to form a soft, pliable dough that is rested before being shaped into thick discs and deep-fried in sesame oil until golden and crisp. The result is a confection with a crispy exterior and a slightly chewy, dense, aromatic interior. Adhirasam is technically gluten-free (pure rice flour base) and vegan. It is particularly associated with Tamil Nadu (Thanjavur region) and is a central Deepavali sweet for Singapore's Tamil community, made in homes and sold at Little India sweet shops during the festive season. Nutritionally, Adhirasam is energy-dense (380 kcal/100g) with a high carbohydrate content (65g/100g), of which 38g is sugar from jaggery. The deep-fried rice flour and jaggery combination creates a high-GI (~70) and high-GL-per-piece (~22.8) sweet. A critical clinical teaching point is the widespread misconception in the Indian community that jaggery is a diabetes-safe alternative to white sugar. While jaggery has marginally higher mineral content (iron 2.5mg/100g, calcium 30mg/100g) compared to refined white sugar, its glycemic index (~68) is nearly identical to white sugar (~65), and it does not confer meaningful glycemic protection for T2DM patients. One Adhirasam piece (50g) delivers approximately 32.5g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~23 — equivalent to a bowl of white rice in glycemic impact.

confidence_reason: Adhirasam is listed directly in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, which is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2. Nutrient values are adopted as reported from this authoritative source. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for rice flour products and peer-reviewed jaggery GI data, with transparent documentation of the estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Adhirasam not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for Adhirasam not reported in ICMR-NIN source. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source; traditional preparation uses sesame oil which contains negligible trans fats). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — Adhirasam is vegan with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source; these vitamins are absent in rice flour and jaggery base). GI of 70 is an analogous estimate: rice flour base GI ~75–80 (University of Sydney GI Database) blended with jaggery GI ~68 (Bhatt et al., 2021, Journal of Food Science and Technology), moderated by fat content from deep-frying. No direct GI measurement for Adhirasam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. FoodEx2 code A039S used as closest defensible parent (fried dough confectionery); no Adhirasam-specific code exists. FoodOn URI set to null: no specific FoodOn concept for Adhirasam; closest parent is deep-fried rice flour sweet. SNOMED CT set to null: no applicable concept. Ethnic tags in original input (Curry, Naan) were incorrect for Adhirasam and have been removed. The is_default flag on the portion was false in the input; corrected to true as this is the only and standard serving size.
